---
title: "CV"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Education

+ PhD Psychology: *The University of Edinburgh* (2012 -- present)
+ MSc Human Cognitive Neuropsychology: *The University of Edinburgh* (2011 -- 2012)
+ BSc (hons) Psychology: *The University of Leeds* (2008 -- 2011)

## Publications

Rhodes, S., Parra, M. A., & Logie, R. H. (2016). Ageing and Feature Binding in Visual Working Memory: The Role of Presentation Time. *The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 69*(4), 654-668. 
<a href="http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/17470218.2015.1038571" target="_blank">[link]</a> <a href="files/RhodesParraLogie2016.pdf" target="_blank" style="color:teal">(pdf)</a>

Brown, L. A., Niven, E. H., Logie, R. H., Rhodes, S., & Allen, R. J. (2016). Visual Feature Binding in Younger and Older Adults: Encoding and Suffix Interference Effects. *Memory*, in press. <a href="http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09658211.2016.1156705" target="_blank">[link]</a> <a href="files/BrownEtAlMemory.pdf" target="_blank" style="color:teal">(pdf)</a>

Rhodes, S., & Parra, M. A. (in press). *Executive Functioning*. Encyclopedia of Geropsychology, Springer.

## In Progress

Parra, M. A., Ibanez, A., Rhodes, S., Baglivo, F., Kargieman, L., Garcia, M. C., Silva, W., Ciraolo, C., Vaucheret, E., Sigman, M., Bekinschtein, T. A., Starr, J., Manes, F., & Della Sala, S. (*under review*). Behavioural and Electrophysiological Correlates of Visual Working Memory Capacity During Change Detection Tasks.

Rhodes, S., Parra, M. A., Cowan, N., & Logie, R. H. (*in prep*). Healthy Ageing and Visual Working Memory: The Role of Mixing Feature and Conjunction Changes.

Parra, M.A., Trujillo, N., Ibanez, A., Mikulan, E., Baglivo, F., Estrada, D., Rhodes, S., Della Sala, S., Manes, F., & Lopera, F. (*in prep*) Event-Related Potentials and Brain Network Signatures of Visual Short-Term Memory Binding Deficits in the Prodromal Stages of Familial Alzheimer’s Disease.

## Selected Posters and Presentations

Rhodes, S., Parra, M. A., Cowan, N., & Logie, R. H. (2016, May). *Healthy Ageing and Binding in Visual Working Memory: Mixed Versus Blocked Designs.* Poster presented at the International meeting of the Psychonomic Society, Granada, Spain. <a href="files/RhodesEtAlGranada.pdf" target="_blank" style="color:teal">(pdf)</a>

Rhodes, S., Parra, M. A., Cowan, N., & Logie, R. H. (2015, Nov). *Visual Working Memory and Change Detection: Reassessing Whole Display Interference.* Poster presented at the annual meeting of the Psychonomic Society, Chicago, Illinois. <a href="files/RhodesEtAlChicago.pdf" target="_blank" style="color:teal">(pdf)</a>

Rhodes, S., Parra, M. A., Cowan, N., & Logie, R. H. (2014, Nov). *The Effect of Age on Visual Working Memory for Features and Conjunctions.* Poster presented at the annual meeting of the Psychonomic Society, Long Beach, California. <a href="files/RhodesEtAlLongBeach.pdf" target="_blank" style="color:teal">(pdf)</a>

Rhodes, S., Parra, M. A., Cowan, N., & Logie, R. H. (2014, Sep). *Does Healthy Ageing Affect the Ability to Bind Features in Visual Working Memory?* Presented at the Seventh European Working Memory Symposium, Edinburgh.

Rhodes, S., Parra, M. A., Cowan, N., & Logie, R. H. (2014, Apr). *Is Feature Binding in Visual Working Memory Age Invariant?* Poster presented at the Cognitive Aging Conference 2014, Atlanta, Georgia. <a href="files/RhodesEtAlAtlanta.pdf" target="_blank" style="color:teal">(pdf)</a>

<br></br>

## Current Projects

### Tablet Assessment of Short-Term Memory Binding

Research assistant on MRC Confidence in Concept (CiC) grant (awarded to Profs. Sergio Della Sala, Robert Logie and Dr Mario Parra) evaluating a tablet version of the short-term memory binding task. Previous versions of this task have been shown to be sensitive to both <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20162428" target="_blank">sporadic</a> and <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25762465" target="_blank">familial</a> Alzheimer's disease. This project will assess the sensitivity and specificity of the portable tablet version with a group of patients with mild-AD and a group of healthy controls. 

### Interaction Effects in Recognition

In recognition experiments it is important to separate out the contribution of an observer's sensitivity from their bias towards a specific response (e.g. a tendency to say same). To do this one has to make some assumptions regarding the process of recognition or the information available to the observer. Popular measures include those that assume a continuous process with a `signal' compared to a criterion (*d-prime*) or those that assume a small number of discrete states (hits minus false-alarms). The choice between measures can greatly affect conclusions regarding differences between groups or conditions with differing bias (as shown <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16097339" target="_blank">here</a> and <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18372758" target="_blank">here</a>). I am working on simulations assessing the effect that choice of measurement has on tests of *interactions*.

### Informed Guessing in Change Detection

In change detection observers are presented with a set of items (e.g. colours) to remember and following a brief delay these items are re-presented with the possibility that *one* item has changed. Assuming observers occasionally don't have enough information to perform the judgment (i.e. some items were not encoded or retained) can they use knowledge of the number of items they have in memory and the number of items presented to inform their guessing? Only one or two studies have assessed this <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21331668" target="_blank">proposal</a>.
